perturbation.original_text: The term “ person ” as used in this letter agreement shall be broadly interpreted to including the media and any corporation, partnership, group, individual or other entity.
perturbation.changed_text: The term “ person ” as used in this letter agreement shall be narrowly interpreted to include only natural persons and not any corporation, partnership, group, or other entity.
perturbation.explanation: The original text defines 'person' broadly. The modified text narrows the definition to exclude corporations and other entities, conflicting with common legal interpretations and potentially limiting the scope of the agreement's protections and obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts 1 U.S. Code § 1, which states that 'In determining the meaning of any Act of Congress, unless the context indicates otherwise… the words “person” and “whoever” include corporations, companies, associations, firms, partnerships, societies, and joint stock companies, as well as individuals.' By explicitly excluding corporations and other entities, the modified text directly conflicts with the statutory definition of 'person'.

Words denoting number, gender, and so forth In determining the meaning of any Act of Congress, unless the context indicates otherwise- words importing the singular include and apply to several persons, parties, or things; words importing the plural include the singular; words importing the masculine gender include the feminine as well; words used in the present tense include the future as well as the present; the words "insane" and "insane person
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts 1 U.S. Code § 1 by narrowly defining 'person' to exclude corporations and other entities, whereas the original text defined it broadly. The scraped law snippet 1 explicitly states that 'In determining the meaning of any Act of Congress, unless the context indicates otherwise- words importing the singular include and apply to several persons, parties, or things'. The modified text's exclusion directly conflicts with this statutory definition, creating a clear contradiction and potentially limiting the agreement's scope in a way not permitted by the general definition of person under the law.

perturbation.original_text: Evaluation Material will be held by such Representatives and kept subject to the terms of this agreement or destroyed to the extent practicable and permitted by law.
perturbation.changed_text: Evaluation Material will be held by such Representatives and kept subject to the terms of this agreement or destroyed at their sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires destruction of Evaluation Material to be 'permitted by law'. The modified text removes this requirement, granting Representatives the freedom to disregard legal restrictions on document retention or destruction, such as those related to litigation holds or regulatory requirements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 1519 - Destruction, alteration, or falsification of records in Federal investigations and bankruptcy
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification conflicts with 18 U.S. Code § 1519, which prohibits the destruction or alteration of records with the intent to impede, obstruct, or influence an investigation or bankruptcy proceeding. The original clause mandates destruction be 'permitted by law', the removal of which allows for the destruction of Evaluation Material even if such action violates the law.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 18 U.S. Code § 1519 - Destruction, alteration, or falsification of records in Federal investigations and bankruptcy | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1519 - Destruction, alteration, or falsification of records in Federal investigations and bankruptcy U.S. Code prev | next Whoever knowingly alters, destroys, mutilates, conceals, covers up, falsifies, or makes a false entry in any record, document, or tangible object with the intent to impede, obstruct, or influence the investigation or proper administration of any matter within the jurisdiction of any department or agency of the United States or any case filed under title 11, or in relation to or contemplation of any such matter or case, shall be fined under this title, imprisoned not more than 20 years, or both. (Added Pub. L. 107–204, title VIII, § 802(a) , July 30, 2002 , 116 Stat. 800 .) U.S. Code Toolbox Law about... perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts 18 U.S. Code § 1519. The original text requires the destruction of Evaluation Material to be 'permitted by law'. The changed text removes this restriction, allowing representatives to destroy material at their 'sole discretion,' even if it violates the law. Both scraped law snippets define 18 U.S. Code § 1519 as prohibiting the 'alter[ation], destroy[ing], mutilat[ion], conceal[ing], cover[ing] up, falsif[ication], or mak[ing] a false entry in any record, document, or tangible object with the intent to impede, obstruct, or influence the investigation or proper administration of any matter'. The removal of the 'permitted by law' clause directly enables actions that could violate this statute, creating a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of litigation relating to this letter agreement, if a court of competent jurisdiction determines in a final, non-appealable order that a party has breached this letter agreement, then such party shall be liable and pay to the non-breaching party the reasonable legal fees incurred by such non-breaching party in connection with such litigation, including any appeal therefrom.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of litigation relating to this letter agreement, the breaching party *may* be liable for the non-breaching party's legal fees, *if the court deems it appropriate*.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing the mandatory 'shall' with the discretionary 'may', and adding the condition of 'if the court deems it appropriate', the provision concerning the payment of legal fees is made ambiguous. This contradicts the original intent of providing a clear and enforceable entitlement to legal fee recovery for the non-breaching party.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1021 - Attorney's fees; determination by agreement; right to specific performance; attorney's fees and costs in action involving claim of monetary sanctions violation
perturbation.law_explanation: Though this contract is governed by the laws of New York, if a similar case were argued in California, this would potentially contradict with California Code of Civil Procedure § 1021, which allows parties to contractually agree on attorney's fees. By making the awarding of attorney's fees discretionary, the modified text weakens the contractual agreement and introduces uncertainty, potentially undermining the parties' agreed-upon allocation of litigation costs. While 1021 permits agreements on fees, it implies a stronger enforceability than the modified text allows. A court may determine the discretion contradicts the implied agreement.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1021. Except as attorney’s fees are specifically provided for by statute, the measure and mode of compensation of attorneys and counselors at law is left to the agreement, express or implied, of the parties; but parties to actions or proceedings are entitled to their costs, as hereinafter provided. (Amended by Stats. 1986, Ch. 377, Sec. 2.)
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates the breaching party 'shall' pay legal fees, creating a clear obligation. The changed text weakens this by stating the breaching party 'may' be liable 'if the court deems it appropriate,' introducing discretion where there was none. This directly undermines the enforceability of the agreement on attorney's fees, as mentioned in the Law Explanation, potentially contradicting the parties' agreed-upon allocation of litigation costs. California Code of Civil Procedure § 1021, provided in Scraped Law Snippet 1, allows parties to contractually agree on attorney's fees. By making the awarding of attorney's fees discretionary, the modified text weakens the contractual agreement.

perturbation.original_text: For purposes of this agreement, (i) ”Significant Event” shall mean (A) the entry by Captaris into an agreement 4 providing for, or the board of directors of Captaris otherwise recommending to the stockholders of Captaris, any merger, sale, stock issuance or other business combination transaction pursuant to which the outstanding shares of Captaris common stock would be converted into cash or securities of another person or 13D Group, or 20% or more of the then outstanding shares of Captaris common stock would be owned by persons other than the then current holders of shares of Captaris common stock, or which would result in all or a substantial portion of Captaris’s assets being sold to any person or 13D Group, or the outstanding shares of Captaris common stock immediately prior to any such transaction would constitute less than 80% of the outstanding shares of Captaris common stock immediately after such transaction, or (B) an unsolicited bona fide tender offer or exchange offer is made by any person or 13D Group to acquire securities of Captaris which would (when added to shares already owned by such person or 13D Group) represent 20% or more of the then outstanding shares of Captaris common stock
perturbation.changed_text: For purposes of this agreement, (i) ”Significant Event” shall mean any change in the weather that the involved parties view as such.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a clear and specific definition of 'Significant Event' related to corporate transactions. The modified text replaces this definition with a vague and subjective criterion ('any change in the weather that the involved parties view as such'). This creates ambiguity and undermines the original intent of the standstill provision.
perturbation.law_explanation: The 'Significant Event' definition is tied to triggering events under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, particularly Section 14(d) which relates to tender offers. By replacing the specific definition with a weather-related one, the standstill provision becomes unenforceable and meaningless in the context of securities law, and introduces ambiguity and uncertainty into a contract meant to be definitive.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: The Laws That Govern the Securities Industry | Investor.gov Skip to main content The .gov means it’s official. Federal government websites often end in .gov or .mil. Before sharing sensitive information, make sure you’re on a federal government site. The site is secure. The https:// ensures that you are connecting to the official website and that any information you provide is encrypted and transmitted securely. Investor.gov U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission Search Investor.gov The Laws That Govern the Securities Industry Note: Except as otherwise noted, the links to the securities laws below are from Statute Compilations maintained by the Office of the Legislative Counsel, U.S. House of Representatives. These links are provided for the user's convenience and may not reflect all recent amendments. If you have questions concerning the meaning or application of a particular law, please consult with an attorney who specializes in securities law. Securities Act of 1933 Securities Exchange Act of 1934 Trust Indenture Act of 1939 Investment Company Act of 1940 Investment Advisers Act of 1940 Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act of 2010 Jumpstart Our Business Startups Act of 2012 Rules and Regulations Securities Act of 1933 Often referred to as the "truth in securities" law, the Securities Act of 1933 has two basic objectives: require that investors receive financial and other significant information concerning securities being offered for public sale; and prohibit deceit, misrepresentations, and other fraud in the sale of securities. See the full text of the Securities Act of 1933 . Purpose of Registration A primary means of accomplishing these goals is the disclosure of important financial information through the registration of securities. This information enables investors, not the government, to make informed judgments about whether to purchase a company's securities. While the SEC requires that the information provided be accurate, it does not guarantee it. Investors who purchase securities and suffer losses have important recovery rights if they can prove that there was incomplete or inaccurate disclosure of important information. The Registration Process In general, securities sold in the U.S. must be registered. The registration forms companies file provide essential facts while minimizing the burden and expense of complying with the law. In general, registration forms call for: a description of the company's properties and business; a description of the security to be offered for sale; information about the management of the company; and financial statements certified by independent accountants. Registration statements and prospectuses become public shortly after filing with the SEC. If filed by U.S. domestic companies, the statements are available on the EDGAR database accessible at www.sec.gov. Registration statements are subject to examination for compliance with disclosure requirements. Not all offering
perturbation.justification: The original contract text provides a specific definition of 'Significant Event' related to corporate transactions and securities law, as described in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. The changed text replaces this with a weather-related definition, which renders the provision meaningless in the context of securities law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that the Securities Act of 1933 requires investors to receive 'financial and other significant information concerning securities being offered for public sale; and prohibit deceit, misrepresentations, and other fraud in the sale of securities.' By changing the definition of 'Significant Event' to a weather-related event, the contract undermines the purpose of disclosing significant financial information related to potential securities transactions, as it introduces ambiguity and uncertainty.
